feedback.text: I decided to break my feedback into sections. Forgive the caps lock. PRODUCTS BUILT TO BREAK Every product that has never needed online connectivity until very recent history is being transformed into always-online products that constantly spy on its users and may at any given moment, be it due to internet outage or due to the manufacturer's whim, be rendered unusable or effectively downgrated. Just this week, beds have been rendered unusable because Amazon's Web Services were offline: https://arstechnica.com/gadgets/2025/10/smart-beds-leave-sleepers-hot-and-bothered-during-aws-outage/ Gym equipment gets its features disabled if it cannot access the internet: https://arstechnica.com/gadgets/2025/07/firmware-update-hinders-echelon-smart-home-gym-equipments-ability-to-work-offline/ Kindle devices have been downgraded with removal of the ability to strip DRM from e-books: https://blog.the-ebook-reader.com/2025/09/23/new-drm-added-to-kindles-with-5-18-5-update-breaking-drm-removal/ - this effectively locks a consumer's books to Amazon and the device, preventing backups. And to add insult to injury, Amazon has been known to remotely remove books from users: https://goodereader.com/blog/electronic-readers/amazon-is-not-to-be-trusted-anymore-with-their-kindle-e-reader These are just a few examples of problems stemming directly from an "always online" (or "never offline") policy, as well as removal of data ownership from users. These aren't merely design flaws, thousands of expert hours have been spent in making sure that these products work WORSE nowadays than they used to decades ago. GAMES FEEDING AND EXPLOITING ADDICTION In the realms of gaming, there are a number of insidious practices. Microtransactions and datamining have paved the way to some of the most exploitative designs in games: A very large amount of free-to-play games are designed with features intended to keep teasing a non-paying user with microtransaction deals to unlock progress quicker, allow more play time or gain a temporary advantage. Other mechanics keep the player's phone buzzing the whole day, constantly reminding them that if they're not playing then they're falling behind. These practices work together to feed addiction and reckless spending. The chests in Clash Royale are an example of both insidious mechanic at once. The player can fill up to 4 chest slots. They take 3, 8, 12, or 24 hours to open for free, with the 3-hour chest being by far the most common one. Once a chest is ready, the phone buzzes to tell you you can open it and to encourage you to play some more to get a new chest. Conversely, you can "accelerate" the opening of a chest by paying some in-game currency (which can be bough with real money). Clash Royale was specifically named only because of my familiarity with it. I could have described similar problems in many other games, such as Pokemon Go! or Candy Crush. EVERYTHING IS TURNING INTO SPYWARE The GDPR has been one of the best regulations regarding privacy to have ever been written. It's elegant, brief and easy to understand. The wording in the regulation is so plain and simple "it should be as easy to deny consent as it is to give it", which is equivalent of saying "it should be as difficult to give consent as it is to deny it". If strictly functional cookies are allowed, then remembering that the user has rejected consent should be mandatory. Coerced consent isn't consent. Even better, require sites to respect the "Do Not Track" HTTP header. That tech has been around even before the GDPR. Generative AI features are being aggressively bundled with every little piece of software. All major proprietary office suites are now offering to do this or that task using AI, making it trivial for a user to unwittingly share private or confidential data with a company whose main business has become datamining and selling Personally Identifiable Information.
